case_name: Compact treatment selection for mixed pesticide and fumigant contamination in surface water for potable use
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for pesticide- and fumigant-impacted surface water intended for drinking water supply under strict effluent limits, limited footprint, and moderate cost constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A peri-urban water utility plans to upgrade an existing compact surface-water treatment plant drawing from a reservoir influenced by agricultural runoff and historical soil-fumigant residues. Monitoring shows recurring tebuconazole and mevinphos spikes during runoff events, while low-level 1,2-dibromoethane is also present and must be controlled to a very low finished-water concentration. The utility has little spare building area, cannot justify a very high-cost membrane facility, and needs a process train that can reliably meet strict drinking-water goals despite seasonal variability.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should consistently meet very low potable-water concentrations for the listed organic contaminants, including reliable control of 1,2-dibromoethane under variable raw-water conditions.
secondary_goals: limit lifecycle cost to a moderate range
avoid treatment trains with excessive concentrate management burden
retain robustness during seasonal runoff-related contaminant spikes

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer conventional solids handling or replaceable media over difficult high-strength liquid concentrate disposal
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: process must tolerate fluctuating pesticide loads and surface-water quality swings while maintaining low finished-water concentrations

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter to protect downstream volatile and adsorptive treatment steps
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish suspended solids and stabilize downstream unit performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: adsorb tebuconazole and mevinphos and provide additional polishing for residual organics
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbial protection for drinking water supply

applicability_rationale: the contamination pattern favors a split-barrier approach because 1,2-dibromoethane is volatile while the pesticides are more suitably controlled by adsorption
air stripping is a strong fit for volatile halogenated organics and avoids the concentrate stream associated with membrane treatment
GAC is widely applied for pesticide control and also adds polishing resilience when runoff events increase organic contaminant loading
compact clarification plus filtration can be configured in a relatively small footprint compared with large conventional basins
this route balances strict effluent needs with moderate cost better than full membrane systems
potential_risks: air emissions from stripping may require off-gas treatment depending on local air requirements and influent concentration variability
higher DOC periods can shorten GAC run length and increase media replacement frequency
if pretreatment performance deteriorates, downstream adsorption capacity and reliability may decline

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: remove particulates and lower oxidant demand from natural organic matter
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: improve water clarity and protect oxidation system performance
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: oxidatively degrade susceptible pesticide compounds that remain after pretreatment
core_unit_functions.biological_or_GAC_polishing: remove oxidation byproducts and provide residual pesticide polishing

applicability_rationale: this train is technically attractive where pesticide degradation is desired in addition to physical removal
AOP can help with pesticides such as organophosphate compounds when properly designed and preceded by NOM control
aeration addresses the volatile fumigant component, while post-polishing reduces transformation products and residual organics
the train remains more compact than full membrane systems and can be engineered into a constrained site
potential_risks: AOP effectiveness depends strongly on oxidant demand, UV transmittance, and reaction kinetics for each contaminant
bromide in source water creates some byproduct management concern for ozone-based systems
process control and monitoring burden are higher than for simpler adsorption-focused routes

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: reduce suspended solids and natural organic matter loading
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect downstream media and maintain stable hydraulics
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_lead_lag: provide primary adsorption of tebuconazole and mevinphos with some organic micropollutant buffering

applicability_rationale: lead-lag GAC is robust for variable pesticide loads and helps buffer event-driven concentration spikes
placing GAC before final stripping can reduce broader organic burden before the volatile polishing stage
this is a feasible alternative when the utility wants strong adsorption redundancy for the pesticide fraction
the route remains technically capable of meeting strict limits if designed with sufficient media contact time and stripping performance
potential_risks: using GAC as the primary barrier ahead of stripping can increase bed replacement frequency because DOC competes for adsorption sites
1,2-dibromoethane control is still dependent on the stripping step and associated off-gas considerations
overall footprint may be somewhat larger than the top-ranked split-barrier arrangement if long empty-bed contact times are needed

core_unit_functions.pretreatment_coagulation_filtration: reduce fouling potential before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide broad rejection of dissolved organic contaminants
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: polish any trace organics and improve taste and odor stability
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust finished water chemistry after membrane treatment

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is technically capable and offers broad-spectrum control across mixed contaminants
this route may be appealing where future unknown contaminants are also a concern
high rejection capability can support strict finished-water targets if concentrate management is available
potential_risks: concentrate disposal is a major mismatch with the stated residuals preference
membrane fouling risk is significant for surface water with moderate DOC and variable turbidity
high operator attention and chemical cleaning requirements weaken fit for this compact but cost-sensitive upgrade

core_unit_functions.compact_clarification_or_inline_coagulat: lower turbidity and reduce oxidant demand
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide clarified water to the oxidation stage
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process_only: attempt degradation of target organic contaminants

applicability_rationale: included as a technically plausible but lower-ranked route because oxidation can contribute to pesticide degradation
compact installation is possible where building space is very constrained
potential_risks: AOP alone is not a preferred standalone barrier for reliable 1,2-dibromoethane control compared with air stripping or other VOC-focused methods
performance for mixed contaminants can be uncertain without downstream adsorption or biological polishing
transformation products and disinfection byproduct interactions may require additional monitoring

preferred_rank_pattern: split-barrier routes that pair VOC control for 1,2-dibromoethane with adsorption or polishing for pesticides should rank near the top
membrane treatment may appear as a technically strong but not first-ranked option because of concentrate and cost penalties
AOP-only configurations should rank lower than trains with dedicated VOC removal and polishing barriers

must_reflect: strict finished-water requirements for mixed organic contaminants
high footprint pressure favoring compact process layouts
moderate capital and operating cost limitations
preference to avoid difficult liquid concentrate residuals

must_contain: why 1,2-dibromoethane treatment favors volatile-organic-control approaches such as air stripping
why tebuconazole and mevinphos can justify GAC or oxidation-plus-polishing treatment
why surface-water DOC and turbidity make pretreatment important
why full membrane options are downgraded despite strong technical capability

should_mention: seasonal runoff spikes in pesticide concentration
off-gas management needs for air stripping of volatile contaminants
GAC exhaustion or reduced bed life under moderate DOC loading
oxidation byproduct or bromide-related concerns where AOP is proposed
